senses.definition: a person who makes shields
examples: The shieldmaker crafted a sturdy bronze shield for the warrior.
In medieval markets, a shieldmaker's workshop was often busy.

senses.definition: a term with no common usage in contemporary English; may be a nonce or erroneous form
examples: Shieldmay appears in some obscure texts but is not used in modern English.
Scholarly editions sometimes annotate shieldmay as a historical variant.

senses.definition: a rare or obsolete term; not found as a common English word
examples: Shieldrake does not appear in modern dictionaries.
Some dialect sources list shieldrake without widely recognized meaning.

senses.definition: plural form of shieldrake; not widely attested in contemporary usage
examples: Old texts mention shieldrakes in passing.
The term shieldrakes appears only in specialized glossaries.

senses.definition: a flat piece of protective equipment carried on the arm or worn on the body to protect against blows
examples: The knight raised his shield to block the strike.
Shields come in different sizes for adults and children.

senses.definition: to protect or defend someone or something from harm
examples: The city shielded its residents from the flood by building levees.
Policies shield workers from exploitation.

senses.definition: a noun that may refer to a creature with a shield-like tail (rare/obsolete) or a constructed term with no common usage
examples: In some natural history catalogs, shieldtail appears as a descriptive name.
shieldtail is not widely used in modern writing.

senses.definition: a defensive line formed by soldiers placing shields together to block an attack
examples: The phalanx closed ranks into a tight shieldwall.
Shieldwall helped the army withstand the assault.

senses.definition: plural of shieldwall; multiple shield-walled formations
examples: Ancient battles often featured shieldwalls as a primary tactic.
Archaeologists uncovered remnants of shieldwalls at the site.

senses.definition: incorrect or archaic spelling of 'shielded'; not standard in modern usage
examples: Shieled doors were described in old manuscripts.
The manuscript consistently uses shieled instead of shielded.

senses.definition: a shelter or hut, typically in a hillside area used for grazing or holiday dwelling in parts of the UK and Ireland
examples: They rented a shieling for the weekend in the highlands.
A simple shieling offered shelter from rain.

senses.definition: plural of shieling; multiple huts or shelters
examples: The valley is dotted with old shielings used by shepherds.
Tourists stayed in renovated shielings during the festival.

senses.definition: plural of shiel; may be a surname or a lesser-used dialect word; not common in modern usage
examples: The village contains several houses with the surname Shiel.

senses.definition: more shy or easily frightened than someone else
examples: Among the crowd, she was the shier sister.
The cat was shier than the others and hid behind the sofa.

senses.definition: plural of shier; not common; could be a surname or dialect plural
examples: Surname records list several people named Shier.

senses.definition: to move away suddenly from something that frightens you; to veer aside
examples: The horse shies at the sound of the trumpet.
She shies away from crowded places.

senses.definition: a sudden movement away from something frightening
examples: With a sharp shie, the horse reared.
His shies during thunderstorms worried the trainer.

senses.definition: most shy or timid
examples: Among the group, she was the shiest student.
He felt the shiest in the crowd and stayed to the back.

senses.definition: a change in position, direction, or focus
examples: He felt a shift in the wind.
There was a shift in public opinion.

senses.definition: to move or cause to move from one place to another; to change position or direction
examples: She shifted her chair closer to the table.
The balance shifted after the new policy was announced.

senses.definition: to change to a different job or role temporarily
examples: He shifted to part-time work last month.
The team shifted responsibilities after the manager left.

senses.definition: the capacity to be shifted or moved; a rarely used technical term
examples: Shiftability refers to how easily components can be relocated.
In engineering, shiftability affects design flexibility.

senses.definition: able to be moved or changed in position
examples: The table is shiftable to fit the room layout.
Shiftable panels allow for flexible space design.

senses.definition: an archaic or rare term; no common contemporary meaning
examples: Some dictionaries list shiftage as a historical measure or term.

senses.definition: past tense of shift; moved or changed position
examples: He shifted his weight to get comfortable.
The game shifted momentum after the goal.

senses.definition: a device or person that shifts something; in cars a transmission component; or in biology a person who shifts shapes (shape-shifter in folklore)
examples: The car's shifter moves the gears smoothly.
In folklore, a shifter can take the form of another creature.

senses.definition: plural of shifter; devices or people that shift; or in slang, people who shift loyalties
examples: The shifters in the workshop adjusted the conveyor.
In fantasy, shifters can change form.

senses.definition: full of shifts or changes; not common in modern English
examples: The plan proved shiftful and required many revisions.

senses.definition: the quality of being shiftful; not a common term in contemporary usage
examples: Scholars debated the shiftfulness of the approach.
